food_name: Sri Lankan Fish Curry (Tuna or Mackerel in Thick Coconut Milk and Goraka Sauce)
aliases: மீன் குழம்பு (Meen Kuzhambu Sri Lanka style)

tamil: மீன் குழம்பு (Meen Kuzhambu Sri Lanka style)

region_of_origin: Sri Lanka — coastal regions (Negombo, Jaffna, Galle)
food_subgroup: Fatty fish curry in coconut milk-goraka sauce; 1 serving ~200g; high protein (~28g per serving); moderate fat (~14g per serving)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Sri Lankan Fish Curry specifically. Closest parent would be a generic fish curry concept. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish curry — closest parent code used; Sri Lankan fish curry with coconut milk and goraka souring agent is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDC is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a fish-based curry dish. No exact code for Sri Lankan goraka fish curry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Sri Lankan Fish Curry. SNOMED CT food coding does not extend to regional curry preparations at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 serving (~200g: fish pieces ~120g + thick curry sauce ~80g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sri Lankan Fish Curry is approximately 200g, comprising roughly 120g of fish pieces (mackerel or tuna) and 80g of thick coconut milk-goraka curry sauce, typically served alongside steamed rice in a Sri Lankan rice-and-curry meal.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lankan Fish Curry — Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Profile (2012) + USDA SR Legacy (fish component)
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) for the composite curry dish and USDA SR Legacy for the mackerel/tuna fish component. Values represent a composite of fish (~60% by weight) and coconut milk-goraka sauce (~40% by weight). Per-100g values are consistent with the described macronutrient profile for a fatty fish in coconut milk curry preparation. Confidence: medium, as Sri Lanka MRI FCP is an approved regional source and USDA SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish Curry — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate fish and coconut milk dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sri Lankan Fish Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~10 is an analogous estimate based on the negligible available carbohydrate content (~3g per 100g, ~6g per 200g serving). The dish is protein- and fat-dominant; available carbohydrate per 200g serving is approximately 5g after fibre subtraction, yielding GL ~0.5. This is consistent with GI values for other low-carbohydrate fish and coconut milk preparations. The estimate is defensible and conservative.

hypertension.guidance: Sri Lankan Fish Curry provides approximately 575mg sodium per standard 200g serving — roughly 25% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals — primarily from added salt and any Maldive fish flakes used in preparation; patients with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~10) and negligible glycemic load (~0.5 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sri Lankan Fish Curry has a GI of approximately 10 and a glycemic load of ~0.5 per 200g serving due to its negligible carbohydrate content, making it an excellent protein- and fat-dominant choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the ~2.4g EPA+DHA omega-3 fatty acids per serving from mackerel may additionally support insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 EPA+DHA content (~2.4g per 200g serving) from fatty fish (mackerel/tuna)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sri Lankan Fish Curry prepared with mackerel provides approximately 2.4g EPA+DHA omega-3 fatty acids per 200g serving, which meets or exceeds the 2g/day threshold associated with clinically meaningful reductions in triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; the moderate saturated fat from coconut milk (~7g per serving) is partially offset by the cardioprotective omega-3 profile, and regular consumption as part of a balanced diet is consistent with cardiovascular health guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The ~2.4g EPA+DHA per serving of Sri Lankan mackerel fish curry falls within the 2.0–3.0g/day therapeutic range studied in rheumatoid arthritis clinical trials, suggesting that regular consumption could provide clinically relevant anti-inflammatory omega-3 supplementation from whole food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sri Lankan Fish Curry (Malu Kariya) is a traditional coastal Sri Lankan dish featuring fatty fish — most commonly mackerel (Indian mackerel, Rastrelliger kanagurta) or tuna — simmered in a thick coconut milk sauce with goraka (dried Garcinia cambogia rind) as the defining souring agent. Unlike South Indian fish curries that use tamarind, Sri Lankan fish curry uses goraka, which imparts an intensely tart, slightly astringent flavour and a deep reddish-brown colour to the sauce. The spice base typically includes turmeric, roasted curry powder (black curry style), green chilli, curry leaf, and mustard seed tempering. Tomatoes may be added for additional acidity and body. Per 200g serving (approximately 120g fish + 80g sauce), the dish provides approximately 310 kcal, 28g protein, 14g fat (including ~2.4g EPA+DHA omega-3 from mackerel), and only 6g carbohydrate, yielding a glycemic index of ~10 and glycemic load of ~0.5 — nutritionally negligible in glycemic terms. Sodium is approximately 575mg per serving. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is a staple of Sri Lankan coastal cuisine in the regions of Negombo, Jaffna, and Galle, and is available at Sri Lankan restaurants in Singapore. Goraka's hydroxycitric acid (HCA) content at culinary doses (~0.5g per serving) is clinically negligible and does not confer weight management properties. The dish is an excellent source of high-quality marine protein and long-chain omega-3 fatty acids, making it nutritionally valuable for cardiovascular health, rheumatoid arthritis management, and blood glucose control in Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: is Sri Lankan fish curry good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from the Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Profile (2012) combined with USDA SR Legacy for the fish component. The Sri Lanka MRI FCP is an approved regional source; USDA SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source. The composite dish values are plausible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on negligible carbohydrate content, not a direct measurement. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact per-100g entry for this specific composite dish preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Sri Lankan fish curry preparation (mackerel/tuna in coconut milk-goraka sauce) is not individually reported in the Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level; individual fatty acid breakdown would require recipe-level calculation from component data, which is beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for a traditional fish and coconut milk preparation with no hydrogenated fats. Goraka (Garcinia cambogia rind) HCA content at culinary doses (~0.5g per serving) is clinically negligible; do NOT attribute weight management properties to this dish based on goraka content. Vitamin A value (15 mcg RAE per 100g) represents retinol activity equivalents estimated from the fish and curry leaf components. Selenium value (25 mcg per 100g) is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for mackerel. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally justified: protein_g 14.0 from fatty fish; fat_g 7.0 from fish fat and coconut milk; carbohydrate_g 3.0 from tomatoes, onion, and spice paste components.
